evaluations.query: Generate an Alembic migration from SQLAlchemy model changes
expected_behavior: Uses alembic revision --autogenerate to detect model changes and generate migrations
Configures async env.py with asyncpg for non-blocking migration execution
Reviews generated SQL before applying to catch incorrect or destructive operations
Sets target_metadata from Base.metadata to enable accurate schema introspection

evaluations.query: Add a NOT NULL column to a large production table
expected_behavior: Uses two-phase approach: nullable first then alter
Backfills data in batches with LIMIT and FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
Creates indexes CONCURRENTLY outside transaction to avoid locking production tables
Implements proper downgrade function for safe rollback of schema changes

evaluations.query: Merge two parallel Alembic migration branches
expected_behavior: Uses alembic merge to combine branch heads
Creates merge revision with tuple down_revision
Uses branch labels for feature isolation
Documents merge point in migration docstring

evaluations.query: Design a normalized database schema for an e-commerce system
expected_behavior: Normalizes to 3NF with separate entity tables
Uses atomic values (no comma-separated lists)
Eliminates transitive dependencies to achieve proper third normal form compliance
Justifies any denormalization with performance evidence

evaluations.query: Optimize slow database queries with proper indexing
expected_behavior: Indexes foreign keys and WHERE clause columns
Uses composite indexes with most selective column first
Chooses correct index type (B-tree, GIN, HNSW)
Considers partial indexes for filtered queries

evaluations.query: Design a MongoDB schema for a content management system
expected_behavior: Chooses embed vs reference based on access patterns
Considers document size limits to prevent exceeding the 16MB BSON maximum
Plans sharding key for even distribution
Defines schema validation rules to enforce data integrity at the collection level

evaluations.query: Set up schema version tracking for a database
expected_behavior: Creates schema_version table with checksums for tracking applied migration integrity
Uses semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) for schema change classification and compatibility
Implements audit trail with row-level versioning or CDC
Tracks applied_at, applied_by, and execution_time for complete migration audit history
Tests upgrade and downgrade cycle locally
Verifies data preservation after rollback to ensure no records are lost
Documents destructive rollbacks in migration docstring
Includes CI pipeline for migration testing

evaluations.query: Detect and prevent schema drift across environments
expected_behavior: Uses checksum verification on deployed migrations
Implements advisory locks for concurrent migration prevention
Configures environment-specific settings (timeouts, locks) for safe production migration execution
Follows local -> CI -> staging -> production flow

evaluations.query: Should I use MongoDB or PostgreSQL for our new microservice? The data is mostly JSON documents.
expected_behavior: Defaults to PostgreSQL unless there is a specific validated reason to choose otherwise
Recommends PostgreSQL JSONB for JSON document workloads covering 90 percent of MongoDB use cases
Evaluates based on project tier from interview through enterprise scale for appropriate tooling
Warns against choosing MongoDB because it is trendy without benchmarking against PostgreSQL JSONB

evaluations.query: How do I set up automated rollback for database migrations in case something goes wrong?
expected_behavior: Recommends pgroll for automated dual-schema versioning with built-in rollback capability
Monitors pg_stat_activity for long-running queries and locks during migration execution
Checks replication lag on replicas before completing the migration with pgroll complete
Follows environment order local to CI to staging to production and never skips stages

evaluations.query: I need to rename a column in our production database without any downtime. How do I do this safely?
expected_behavior: Uses expand-contract pattern: add new column, migrate data, remove old column in separate phases
Never uses ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN directly as it locks the table during metadata change
Creates indexes with CONCURRENTLY to avoid locking tables during index creation on large tables
Adds constraints with NOT VALID first then validates separately to avoid blocking writes
